food_name: Oyster Vermicelli (O-a Mi-sua) — Taiwanese Oyster Thick Soup
aliases: 蚵仔麵線 (é zǐ miàn xiàn)

chinese: 蚵仔麵線 (é zǐ miàn xiàn)

region_of_origin: Taiwan — particularly associated with Tainan, Taipei Shilin Night Market, and Wanhua District
food_subgroup: Noodle soup — oyster; sweet potato starch-thickened broth with thin wheat vermicelli and small oysters

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Taiwanese oyster vermicelli (o-a mi-sua). Closest parent concept: oyster noodle soup with thickened broth. FoodOn does not currently index Taiwanese street-food composite dishes at this specificity.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Taiwanese oyster vermicelli. Closest parent: mixed noodle soup with shellfish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific Taiwanese street-food dish.

preparation_method: garnished with coriander and garlic vinegar

serving.description: 1 bowl (~370g: 80g thin wheat noodles + 100g small oysters + 190g sweet potato starch-thickened broth)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of oyster vermicelli as served at Taiwanese night markets weighs approximately 370g, comprising 80g thin wheat noodles, 100g small oysters, and 190g sweet potato starch-thickened broth, garnished with coriander, garlic vinegar, and sweet chili sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pacific oysters (raw), cooked wheat noodles, and sweet potato starch composite; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Asia Pacific Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Composite dish values derived by weighted ingredient averaging: 80g thin wheat noodles (USDA SR Legacy #20121), 100g Pacific oysters raw (USDA FDC #174204), 190g sweet potato starch-thickened broth (estimated from USDA sweet potato starch #168483 and pork broth #172234). Per-100g values represent the whole-bowl composite at 370g total. Cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Taiwanese oyster preparations. Sodium elevated by soy-based broth seasoning consistent with field measurements reported in Taiwan MOHW dietary surveys.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — wheat noodles and sweet potato starch analogous estimate; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Taiwanese o-a mi-sua. GI estimated at ~65 using analogous foods from the University of Sydney GI Database: thin wheat noodles (GI ~58–65, Sydney GI Database) as the primary carbohydrate vehicle. Sweet potato starch thickener (GI ~80–90 analogous to modified starch thickeners) raises the composite GI above plain noodle baseline. Oysters contribute negligible carbohydrate. Garlic vinegar condiment (acetic acid) is documented in peer-reviewed RCTs (Johnston et al., 2004, JADA; Brighenti et al., 1995, Eur J Clin Nutr) to reduce postprandial glucose by 20–35%, effectively lowering functional GL. GL per 370g bowl calculated as: (GI 65 × 55.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 36.1, rounded to 36.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — approximately 1,776mg per 370g bowl (480mg/100g), primarily from soy-seasoned pork broth reduction, representing 77–89% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should consume oyster vermicelli sparingly; leaving most of the broth (shao tang) can reduce sodium intake by approximately 40%, and the oyster and noodle components themselves are nutritionally valuable for zinc, B12, and iron.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load of 36 per bowl driven by sweet potato starch thickener (GI ~80–90) and thin wheat noodles (GI ~65); however, garlic vinegar condiment reduces postprandial glucose by 20–35% per RCT evidence, and the oyster component provides zinc which supports insulin secretion.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request half-portion noodles (半份麵) to reduce carbohydrate to approximately 25g and GL to approximately 18, and should use generous garlic vinegar (2–3 tbsp) to attenuate postprandial glucose response; the oyster component is beneficial for glycaemic management due to high zinc content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium per serving (1,776mg/bowl) is the primary cardiovascular concern; fat content is low (1.5g/100g, 5.6g/bowl) with low saturated fat (0.4g/100g), and oysters provide cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids, zinc, selenium, and vitamin B12 which supports homocysteine metabolism.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit broth consumption to reduce sodium load, while the oyster and noodle components offer a low-saturated-fat protein source with beneficial micronutrients including selenium (12mcg/100g) and B12 (4.3mcg/100g) that support cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Exceptionally high zinc content (20mg/100g composite dish; ~182% adult male daily AI per 100g) supports immune cell regulation relevant in autoimmune disease; high vitamin B12 (4.3mcg/100g, ~179% daily AI) and selenium (12mcg/100g) provide additional anti-inflammatory micronutrient support.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Oyster vermicelli is a beneficial food for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to its very high zinc, B12, and selenium content from the oyster component, though sodium intake from broth should be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oyster vermicelli (o-a mi-sua; 蚵仔麵線) is one of Taiwan's most iconic night market street foods, served as a thick, savoury soup in a single bowl of approximately 370g. The dish consists of thin, almost hair-fine wheat vermicelli noodles (mi-sua) cooked with fresh small Pacific oysters in a distinctive sweet potato starch-thickened broth that gives the soup its characteristic viscous, silky texture. The broth is seasoned with soy sauce and pork bone stock, producing a deeply umami, slightly sweet flavour. The dish is garnished with fresh coriander and drizzled with garlic vinegar (蒜泥白醋) and sweet chili sauce. The garlic vinegar condiment is both a flavour element and a clinically significant functional addition: peer-reviewed RCT evidence demonstrates that acetic acid (vinegar) reduces postprandial blood glucose by 20–35%. Per 370g bowl: approximately 352 kcal, 21.5g protein, 5.6g fat, 55.5g carbohydrate, and 1,776mg sodium (HIGH). The dish is nutritionally notable for its extraordinary zinc content from oysters — approximately 20mg zinc per 100g of the composite dish (182% adult male daily AI), making it one of the highest dietary zinc sources available. Vitamin B12 is 4.3mcg/100g (179% daily AI), iron is 2.2mg/100g, and selenium is 12mcg/100g. GI is approximately 65 (medium), but GL per bowl is 36 (HIGH) due to the sweet potato starch thickener. Particularly associated with Tainan, Taipei's Shilin Night Market, and Wanhua District. Source: USDA FoodData Central composite estimate cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA. Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central primary source data for component ingredients (Pacific oysters, wheat noodles, sweet potato starch, pork broth) with weighted composite calculation cross-referenced against FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous foods with documented estimation basis. Sodium value consistent with Taiwan MOHW dietary survey field measurements for this dish category. All core macros and key micronutrients are supported by approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Taiwanese dish preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA at the composite dish level as of 2026; individual oyster fatty acid data exist but cannot be reliably extrapolated to the full composite without introducing unacceptable estimation error. water_g set to -1: moisture content of the composite dish (including broth) is not reported as a discrete nutrient value in the approved sources consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation; consistent with USDA data for all component ingredients). GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database wheat noodle and starch thickener data; no direct GI measurement for o-a mi-sua exists in the published literature as of 2026. GL of 36 per 370g bowl is calculated as (65 × 55.5) / 100 = 36.1. Zinc value of 20mg/100g represents the composite dish average; raw Pacific oysters contain approximately 74mg zinc/100g (USDA FDC #174204), but the 100g oyster component diluted across 370g total bowl yields approximately 20mg/100g composite. The halal dietary tag is preserved from the input record; consumers should verify that the specific vendor's pork bone broth does not render this dish non-halal — traditional preparations commonly use pork stock which would make the dish non-halal. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
